coredata.dc:title: Eco-friendly ink formulation of column purified carbon dots from GABA for anticounterfeiting applications

coredata.dc:description: Forgery of valuable products causes a negative impact on the society as well as the economy of the country. There is a growing demand to not only differentiate or authenticate genuine documents/products but also to protect their integrity. Carbon dots (CDs) are a class of fluorescent nanomaterials that are well-known for their facile synthesis, good photostability and less toxicity profile. The current research work focuses on the preparation of CDs via hydrothermal method using γ-aminobutyric acid (GABA) that contains amino and carboxylic groups. Column chromatography technique is adopted to purify the GABA-derived CDs from the reactants and by-products. The fourth fraction obtained after column purification containing CDs with fluorescence emission in the visible region is chosen for further studies. The presence of spherical CDs confirmed through TEM imaging are chemically characterised using SAED, EDS, DLS, FTIR, XPS, Raman and XRD spectroscopy. The blue (λem = 490 nm) and green (λem = 538 nm) emitting CDs present in the fourth fraction displayed a fluorescence lifetime of 1.90 ns and 2.02 ns. Theoretical studies are performed using B3LYP/6-31G(d,p) theory level on different plausible structures. The HOMO-LUMO band gap of 2.3 eV deduced using DFT calculation is in close agreement with the optical band gap of 2.6 eV derived from Tauc plot. A complex forming mechanism is proposed for the fluorescence quenching of CDs upon examining the EDS data of the precipitate obtained upon addition of cupric ions. Further, the CDs are used as pigments to formulate a water-based ink for flexographic printing on UV-dull paper substrate. The printed samples exhibited good colorimetric values, lightfastness, and rub resistance. The security features of the ink film include an UV-induced yellow fluorescence, which will be known to the forger and a secondary quenching of fluorescence when exposed to cupric ions, which can be used by the user to validate document/product authenticity. This stimulus responsive optical property is also explored in the design of ionochromic security paper based on cupric ion induced fluorescence quenching of CDs for data storage and decryption. Moreover, the lesser surface roughness and electrical parameter values obtained for the print proofs could be further explored for the potential application of CD-derived ink in electronic anticounterfeiting.

A) Zeta potential histogram, and TEM images of CDs under b) lower and c) higher magnifications. d) TEM and e) STEM images displaying numerous carbon nanorods. f) TEM-EDS of CDs and g) HAADF image showing elemental distribution. individual distribution of h) C, i) N and j) O.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Forgery of valuable products causes a negative impact on the society as well as the economy of the country. There is a growing demand to not only differentiate or authenticate genuine documents/products but also to protect their integrity. Carbon dots (CDs) are a class of fluorescent nanomaterials that are well-known for their facile synthesis, good photostability and less toxicity profile. The current research work focuses on the preparation of CDs via hydrothermal method using γ-aminobutyric acid (GABA) that contains amino and carboxylic groups. Column chromatography technique is adopted to purify the GABA-derived CDs from the reactants and by-products. The fourth fraction obtained after column purification containing CDs with fluorescence emission in the visible region is chosen for further studies. The presence of spherical CDs confirmed through TEM imaging are chemically characterised using SAED, EDS, DLS, FTIR, XPS, Raman and XRD spectroscopy. The blue (λem = 490 nm) and green (λem = 538 nm) emitting CDs present in the fourth fraction displayed a fluorescence lifetime of 1.90 ns and 2.02 ns. Theoretical studies are performed using B3LYP/6-31G(d,p) theory level on different plausible structures. The HOMO-LUMO band gap of 2.3 eV deduced using DFT calculation is in close agreement with the optical band gap of 2.6 eV derived from Tauc plot. A complex forming mechanism is proposed for the fluorescence quenching of CDs upon examining the EDS data of the precipitate obtained upon addition of cupric ions. Further, the CDs are used as pigments to formulate a water-based ink for flexographic printing on UV-dull paper substrate. The printed samples exhibited good colorimetric values, lightfastness, and rub resistance. The security features of the ink film include an UV-induced yellow fluorescence, which will be known to the forger and a secondary quenching of fluorescence when exposed to cupric ions, which can be used by the user to validate document/product authenticity. This stimulus responsive optical property is also explored in the design of ionochromic security paper based on cupric ion induced fluorescence quenching of CDs for data storage and decryption. Moreover, the lesser surface roughness and electrical parameter values obtained for the print proofs could be further explored for the potential application of CD-derived ink in electronic anticounterfeiting.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Supplementary data provided. The datasets generated during and/or analyzed during the current study are available from the first author on reasonable request.

ce:section.ce:para: The CDs are ‘zero-dimensional’ carbonaceous materials smaller than 10 nm, with a sp2 hybridized carbon rich core within a matrix of sp3 carbon groups, and generally surrounded by an amorphous shell of polar functional groups. After the serendipitous discovery of CDs in 2004, they have been intensively studied for several potential applications such as sensing, biolabeling, bioimaging [1–3], drug delivery [4], nanoencryption [5,6], photocatalyst [7,8], light emitting [9,10] and optoelectronic devices [11,12]. They display captivating optical properties including excitation dependent emission, tunable photoluminescence, resistance to photobleaching, and large two-photon excitation cross-sections, in addition to other attractive features such as low toxicity profile and biocompatibility. Generally, CDs are known to exhibit blue or green emission [13–15], whereas in some cases emissions extended towards higher wavelength or near-infrared region are also reported [16–20]. Though CDs with desirable characteristics can be prepared using top-down and bottom-up methods, the bottom-up approaches are extensively preferred as they yield nanocarbonaceous materials with minimal surface defects and superior emission features [8,21]. Hence, hydrothermal/solvothermal processes, pyrolysis or microwave-assisted treatments are used to prepare CDs from both synthetic and natural precursor materials [22]. Moreover, incorporation of heteroatoms can induce enhanced photoluminescence features in these small carbonaceous materials [23].

Biologically active precursors rich in proteins and amino acids serve as carbon (C), nitrogen (N), oxygen (O), and sulphur (S) reservoirs and are attractive raw materials for obtaining CDs with superior emission characteristics [24]. Abundantly available amino acids that possess amine and carboxyl group can be eco-friendly, renewable, inexpensive, and non-toxic sources for preparing N-doped CDs with improved fluorescence properties. There are a few attempts reported on the preparation of CDs from aspartic acid, glutamic acid (acidic amino acids), lysine, arginine, histidine (basic amino acids), phenyl alanine, tryptophan, tyrosine (aromatic amino acids), cysteine, methionine (sulphur containing amino acids), glycine, leucine, proline, serine, and asparagine mostly using hydrothermal method (180–300 ℃, 2–24 h) and in presence of glucose/citric acid (700–1000 W, 1–4 min) and citric acid/H3PO4, using microwave and pyrolysis methods [25–33]. It is well established from these reports that the optical features of CDs including emission colour, excitation wavelength-dependent fluorescence, and quantum yield are influenced by the structure of these proteinaceous amino acids.

Counterfeiting poses a significant global challenge across all sectors including governments, industries, and businesses. Consumer safety is jeopardised by forged items and has serious effects on the society. Therefore, it is extremely critical to identify/authenticate genuine goods, which can be generally achieved using the security features tagged to a product or document [34]. Security printing inks based on CDs display several advantages including simple design, high fluorescence, low toxicity profile, and easy handling, which could be exploited for fast identification of counterfeiting in food/pharmaceuticals safety, data storage, and information encryption/decryption applications. Majority of these anticounterfeiting approaches described by different CDs generally rely on the fluorescence emission colour of the respective aqueous solutions that can be visualized and authenticated upon excitation using a specific wavelength of light such as UV, NIR or laser [35]. The aqueous solution of green CDs derived from plant biomass [36–41], food [42–45], and waste materials [46–51] are reported as invisible security inks. They are either handwritten or inkjet printed on paper for data encryption and decryption by observing mostly blue fluorescence under 365 nm light, except a red emission perceived from Bougainvillea leaves-derived CDs [52] with low quantum yield and have low level security feature. It is also worth noting that the more commonly an overt security technique is utilised, the more appealing it becomes for counterfeiters to tackle it [53]. However, CDs obtained from synthetic materials as precursors generally display superior security features including excitation-wavelength dependent fluorescence [54,55], water induced fluorescence [42], chemiluminescence [56], room temperature phosphorescence [57,58], and triple-mode (fluorescence, up-conversion fluorescence, and room temperature phosphorescence) [59].

Despite above-mentioned reports in using different types of luminescent CDs to develop various security elements, the practical use of a typical ink formulation from CDs and the stimuli dependent fluorescence to authenticate a genuine print obtained through laboratory flexography printing process have not been explored. Moreover, there are only a few studies on the use of purified CDs with optimized optical properties for anticounterfeit application [60]. In the present study, a facile one-step hydrothermal synthesis of green CDs from γ-aminobutyric acid (GABA), with a linear molecular formula of NH2(CH2)3COOH is reported. GABA is a naturally occurring non-protein essential amino acid and an inhibitory neurotransmitter, produced by glutamic acid decarboxylase, uniquely located in neurons. The CDs are used to obtain a water-based ink for flexographic printing on UV-dull paper substrate. Analyses of print characteristics validated good colorimetric values, lightfastness, and rub resistance of the ink film. The quenching of yellow fluorescence of the print upon treatment with Cu2+ ions can be a unique feature for genuine document/product authentication by the user, whereas this feature will be masked to the forger. This unique stimulus responsive optical property is explored in the design of ionochromic security papers for data storage. Moreover, the surface morphology and electrical characteristics of the print proofs are examined for the potential application of CD-derived ink in electronic anticounterfeiting.

GABA (200 mg) was dissolved in 7 mL deionised water. Further, 40% NaOH solution (0.8 g in 2 mL water) and 1 mL acetone was added and transferred into a 25 mL Teflon coated autoclave. The mixture was maintained at 175 °C for 7 h, cooled and filtered. The filtrate was allowed to pass through a 150 mm long and 35 mL column (ABG Initiative), prepared using wet packing technique using 400–700 meshwork silica gel (SRL Chemicals). Dichloromethane (DCM) and methanol was chosen as the solvent system based on the separation observed in thin layer chromatography (TLC) plates.

The fractions obtained after column chromatography were analysed using Fourier transform Infrared (FTIR), UV–Visible and fluorescence spectroscopic techniques. The CDs of interest were further characterized using Dynamic Light Scattering (DLS), X-ray powder Diffraction (XRD), Selected Area Electron Diffraction (SAED), Raman Spectroscopy (RS), Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM), Energy Dispersive X-ray Spectroscopy (EDS), elemental mapping, X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS), and Time Correlated Single Photon Count (TCSPC) techniques.

The optical characterization of CDs was accomplished by recording the UV–visible absorption spectra (1800 Shimadzu UV–visible spectrophotometer), and emission spectra (JASCO spectrofluorometer FP-8300). For analysis of various samples, the excitation and emission bandwidth were varied between 2.5 and 5 nm. The excitation source provided was between 370 and 520 nm and respective emissions were recorded from 300 to 800 nm. The lifetime values of CDs were measured using a DeltaFlex TCSPC Lifetime Fluorimeter with a conventional PMT design. Mai Tai Laser (λex = 340–510 nm) was used as a short-pulse oscillator excitation source.

The structural characterization of the CDs were realized using FTIR spectra (Shimadzu-IR Spirit analyzer, 40 scans at 4 cm−1 resolution), X-ray diffraction patterns (Malvern PANalytical, Empyrean 3rd Gen, Cu Kα source of 1.540598 Å, 2θ range from 10°–80°), Raman spectra (Renishaw Compact Raman Spectrometer, continuous scan mode, excitation at 785 nm, laser power reduced to 0.05%), and XPS spectra (Omicron Nanotechnology Ltd, Germany, Mg-Kα as excitation source, scan range of 60.6–1253.6 eV). The XPS spectra were acquired as a survey and high-resolution scan of individual elements. The JEM 2100F model with an electron source of 160 kV effective resolution was used for TEM imaging, whereas TEM-EDS, SAED, and elemental mapping were achieved using Talos F200 S model. Image-J software was used to process the images. Horiba Scientific Nano Particle analyzer SZ-100 was used to determine the surface charge of the CDs. The elemental analysis was performed using Carl Zeiss EVO 18 analytical scanning electron (SEM) equipped with an Energy Dispersive X-Ray Spectrometer (EDS).

Computational studies were carried out to accomplish a reliable representation of the CDs and to predict the IR and electronic spectra. To aid the analysis of experimental results, the density functional theory (DFT) calculations were performed on CD analogous structure using B3LYP theory and Gaussian type 6-31G(d,p) basis set. The structures of the CDs were optimized followed by the harmonic frequency calculation to confirm the structure at the minimum of the potential energy surface. The UV–Vis spectra of CDs were calculated using vertical excitation of optimized molecule in the ground electronic state. In the process, one hundred possible exited states were considered. For the emission spectrum, the structures of CDs were optimized in the first excited state, and the transition wavelength was used as the emission wavelength. All the calculations were carried out in the solvent (methanol) environment. Gaussian 09 program suite [61] was utilized for all the calculations.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of ink and study of print properties
ce:section.ce:para: The water-based ink for flexography printing was formulated through homogenous mixing of CDs distributed in deionised water (pigment concentrate), glycerol, surfactant, defoamer and wax. The ink composition is presented in Table 1 .

The grammage (in gsm) of UV dull paper substrate was measured using a digital GSM tester. The K bar coater (Bar number = 0) was used to initially coat the ink on the substrate. Paper samples were printed using a RK Flexiproof 100 instrument (50 m/min), equipped with a 9.5 BCM Anilox roll, chambered doctor blade inking system, and a photopolymer plate. The density, L*, a*, and b* readings were recorded using the Xrite i1 Pro spectrophotometer, whereas the abrasion resistance of the ink film on printed paper sample was checked using Sutherland ink rub tester by applying 2 lbs weight. Flex-Axiom Atomic Force Microscope, M/s Nanosurf was used to obtain the surface roughness of the ink films on the substrate and Gwyddion-2.60 software was used to analyse the data to produce a 2D and 3D topological picture of the sample. The Keithley Hall Effect setup at 3706 A was used to measure the electrical properties of the prints on flexible paper substrate at room temperature.

GABA was selected as the precursor material due to its natural availability, and presence of amino and carboxyl groups in its structural framework. Upon subjecting to hydrothermal reaction, these functional groups in GABA can easily undergo cross-linking through dehydration and condensation polymerization processes [62]. Moreover, the heteroatoms, particularly N could get doped into the carbon structure of the GABA-derived CDs, thereby influencing their luminescence properties. Literature evidence suggest hydrothermal treatment at various temperatures (180–300 ℃) for different durations (2–24 h), mostly with glucose or citric acid as passivating agents to obtain CDs of 2–15 nm from amino acid precursors [25,27–30]. Therefore, synthesis reactions to achieve the mother liquor (ML) of GABA-derived CDs were performed for 1 h (ML-1), 3 h (ML-3), 5 h (ML-5) and 7 h (ML-7) by maintaining the temperature at 175 ℃. Upon hydrothermal treatment, the GABA molecules might assemble due to hydrogen bonding. Further, they could undergo dehydration, polymerization, nucleation, carbonization, and finally growth of CDs [63].

Aliquots of mother liquor (ML) obtained from each of these reactions performed at four different temperatures were subjected to absorbance measurements. The spectral traces as presented in Supplementary Fig. 1 (SF1) depict two peaks of unequal intensity belonging to higher (π → π*) and lower (n → π*) transitions. The peaks corresponding to both the transitions remain the same at 267 nm and 304 nm for ML-1 and ML-3 samples. However, a significant spectral shift of 20 nm was perceived for n → π* transitions in the ML-5 and ML-7 samples. The shift in absorbance wavelength suggests the different nature of surface groups that could be present on the CDs. Further, all the four ML samples (MLs-1, 3, 5 and 7) were subjected to column chromatography to analyse the constituent fractions.

As the MLs (ML-1, 3, 5 & 7) obtained after the hydrothermal reactions could usually comprise mixtures of different CD fractions along with the molecular intermediates and by-products, their careful separation and purification is crucial for optimisation of reaction conditions, and isolation of pure CDs possessing desired functional properties. The MLs were initially subjected to TLC to select the appropriate solvent-elution system; beginning from non-polar DCM and gradually increasing the polarity using methanol, and the separation was monitored. Based on the best separations, the following solvent systems were chosen; 8:2 (DCM:Methanol), 7:3 (DCM:Methanol), 6:4 (DCM:Methanol) and 7:3 (Methanol:DCM). Subsequently, chromatographic separations were achieved by loading few aliquots of ML-1, 3, 5 & 7 on to the column. After ensuring complete adsorption, first elution system (8:2 DCM:Methanol) was added and allowed to run through the column. Different fractions were collected eventually upon increase in polarity of the solvent. The number of purified fractions varied; 6 for ML-1 and ML-5, whereas 5 for ML-3 and ML-7, respectively. The photographs of the respective columns shined with 365 nm light source as well as the fractions obtained under daylight and 365 nm UV light source are shown in SF2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical studies of the purified fractions
ce:section.ce:para: UV spectra of the MLs were compared with that obtained for the purified fractions. The 1st, 2nd, 3rd, and 5th fractions displayed no significant shifts in the absorption compared to ML-1, whereas ∼10 nm red shift in the 4th and 6th fractions was observed (for the peak at ∼302 nm compared to ML-1 and other fractions) as displayed in SF3a. The absorption spectra of all the five purified fractions obtained after 3 h of hydrothermal reaction did not deviate much compared to that of ML-3 (SF3b). The absorptions of the 4th and 6th fractions were closer to that of ML-5 as perceived from SF3c. On the contrary, the absorption spectra of the fractions obtained from ML-7 (SF3d) displayed minimal shift in case of 2nd, 3rd 4th fractions, whereas the 1st and especially 5th fractions showed marked blue shifts in their higher wavelength absorptions.

After the comparative analysis of the absorptions of MLs and all the fractions collected after purification, detailed analysis of the emissions of these CD solutions was performed to select the fraction with suitable fluorescence properties. The 3D plots presented in Fig. 1 depict the comparative excitation and emission features of different MLs and their respective purified fractions. The SF4-SF7 represent the emission spectra recorded for ML-1, 3, 5 and 7 and their purified fractions. Moreover, the Supplementary Table 1 (ST1) compares the fluorescence data of the unpurified MLs obtained after different durations of hydrothermal reaction and the respective fractions separated through column chromatography. All the MLs exhibited a common excitation at 440 nm with bluish-green emissions. In ML-1 and ML-5, i) the fractions 1 and 2 were majorly UV to violet/blue emissive, ii) fractions 3, 4 and 5 showed three emissions respectively in UV, violet/blue and green regions, and iii) the last fraction displayed dual emissions producing blue and green fluorescence. The same trend (UV, violet/blue and green emissions) was observed for fractions 1–3 in the case of ML-3 and ML-7. Interestingly, fraction 4 obtained from ML-7 had only blue and green emitters having comparable emission intensity, in contrast to all the other studied samples, wherein the population (intensity) of the blue emitters in comparison to green emitters presented a large variation (Fig. 1). Therefore, the CDs from fourth fraction obtained through purification of ML-7 was chosen for anticounterfeit applications, and hence subjected to detailed characterization.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of GABA-derived CDs of interest
ce:section.ce:para:
After the synthesis and purification of CDs as presented in Fig. 2
a, FTIR characterization was performed to obtain insights into the presence of probable functional groups in the unpurified ML-7 and purified fractions (Fig. 2 b). The precursor material GABA displayed characteristic peaks at 882, 1380 & 1508 cm−1 attributed to –CH2, O-H and N–H bending vibrations. The C-OH and CO stretching vibrations were observed at 1043 and 1684 cm−1, whereas the CH (CH2) stretching was perceived at 2881 cm−1 and 2972 cm−1. In addition, the stretching vibrations of NH2 and COOH groups appeared collectively at 3315 cm−1 [64]. The FTIR spectrum recorded for ML-7 showed peaks at 1046, 1642 and 3307 cm−1, which can be attributed to the stretching vibrations of COH, CO and NH/COOH groups, which was also observed in the IR spectrum of GABA. The peak values of the IR spectra collected for all the purified fractions are depicted in Table 2 . The first, second and third fractions displayed similar peaks, and the stretching vibrations belonging to COOH appears at higher frequency around 3600 cm−1, probably due to the absence of hydrogen bonding with DCM as the solvent. In addition, the last two fractions showed C-OH bending and C-O stretch at 1017 cm−1 and 1112 cm−1 respectively. The fourth and fifth fractions displayed almost similar vibrational peaks for CN, CO, COH, CH, NH as that of the precursor material.

The surface functional groups were further confirmed by analysing the XPS data in combination with a deeper understanding of the elemental composition and the type of hybridisation present in CDs. In the survey spectra as presented in Fig. 2 c, the typical peaks for C (C1s), N (N1s) and O (O1s) are recorded at 285.2 eV, 401.3 eV and 532.1 eV, respectively [62]. In the full scan spectrum (Fig. 2 d) for C1s, four Gaussian peaks were fitted and assigned at 284.8, 285.5, 286.7 and 288.3 eV corresponding to CC/CC, CN/CO, CO and COOH respectively. Similar deconvolution of O1s spectra (Fig. 2 e) revealed two peaks at 531.3 and 532.4 eV belonging to CO and CO. Though the full scan spectra (Fig. 2 f) for N1s appears as noise, peaks were fitted and positioned at 398.4 and 400.1 eV mainly for amino N and pyrrolic N. Further, the elemental quantification was deduced by component ratio analysis using XPS. The N content was 3.29%, when compared to C (67.86%) and O (28.84%) as other constituent elements.

The surface charge decides the tendency of the CDs to coagulate, and hence its dispersion stability. Hence the zeta potential of the CDs was checked using DLS experiment by dispersing them in deionised water and the value was found to be −21.2 mV (Fig. 3
a), indicating the presence of ionizable, negatively charged moieties like carboxylic groups. Though the value obtained is not within the general colloidal stability range (−30 to + 30 mV), the particles did not precipitate even after storage for 4 months due to electrical stabilisation through mutual repulsion. The CDs were further examined via TEM and the images displayed spherical morphology with diameter ranging between 5 and 14 nm ( Fig. 3 b & c). In addition to CDs, Carbon nanorods were also observed and imaged as depicted in Fig. 3 d. Further, to obtain better imageries of nanorods, STEM (Scanning transmission electron microscope) imaging was preformed using HAADF (High angle annular dark field) detector in a conventional TEM mode. Fig. 3 e showcases nanorods with better contrast at a lower magnification [65]. Furthermore, elemental mapping (TEM-EDS) was performed over the same area to examine the distribution of constituent elements. Though it is not a quantitative estimation as the intensity of components may differ, the EDS spectra revealed C as the abundant element (Fig. 3 f), and the presence of O as well as lesser percentage of N, Calcium (Ca) and Sodium (Na). The HAADF image showing the elemental distribution is depicted in Fig. 3 g. As observed in Fig. 3 h–j, C and O are abundantly populated, while N is in minimal concentration.

The Raman spectrum recorded using a 785 nm excitation source as shown in Fig. 4
a revealed two distinct characteristic bands; a sharp peak at 124 cm−1, which could be accounted for the radial breathing mode vibration of carbon nano rods [66,67], and a broad peak at 1388 cm−1 assigned as the D-band or distorted band that arises due to the presence of disordered structures. The vibrational mode for D-band could be mainly attributed to A1g or zone-edge breathing vibration [68,69]. Moreover, peak at 1509 cm−1 is obtained corresponding to G-band due to presence of sp2 hybridised C. Previous literature reports on CDs suggest the appearance of broad peak in XRD indicating their amorphous nature [66]. The XRD peaks obtained for the GABA-derived CDs were closely examined for the possible presence of different allotropes of C (Fig. 4 b). It can be established that intense peaks for both graphitic and diamond C contribute to peaks in XRD spectra. Hence, the experimental data is overlaid with graphite-2H and diamond (C). The peaks at 25.8, 43.0, 44.1 and 50.2 fall in proximity with graphitic C (Fig. 4 bi). The peaks of diamond C are observed at 31.6, 31.9 and 32.5 (Fig. 4 bii). In addition, peaks centred at 29.0 and 29.5 indicate the presence of supercubane as they match well with the reference (Fig. 4 biii). The peak at 31.0 remains un-indexed and the sample peak lie closer to diamond C belonging to diamond allotropic form. Further, the SAED image obtained as bright spots over a selected area during TEM imaging, and the respective ring diffraction pattern indicates the polycrystalline nature of CDs [70,71]. Moreover, the d-space value estimated from the ring diffraction pattern matches with the XRD patterns. The ring diffraction is labelled with the respective plane of diffraction (Fig. 4 c). On the contrary, SAED pattern recorded for certain regions presented diffused rings (Fig. 4 d) suggesting the amorphous nature of CDs [72].

Furthermore, the fluorescence lifetimes of the CDs were recorded using TCSPC in both liquid and solid states. As CDs in liquid state showed emission at 490 and 538 nm (ST1), their corresponding average lifetime was computed. The solution of CDs exhibited a bi-exponential decay a shown in Fig. 5
a with average lifetimes (τavg) of 1.90 ns for blue emitters (λex = 460 nm and λem = 490 nm) and 2.02 ns for green emitters (λex = 460 nm and λem = 538 nm). To measure the solid-state emission and fluorescence lifetime, the CDs were dispersed in deionised water and coated on UV dull paper. The 3D fluorescence spectrum of the blank paper (SF8a) was recorded as the control, which displayed emissions at 430 and 534 nm for excitation at 340 nm. On the contrary, the 3D plots for CDs coated on paper sample (SF8b) showed an additional peak at 574 nm. A red shift of ∼40 nm to display a yellow emission from CDs in the solid-state was observed as opposed to 537 nm to exhibit a green emission in its solution form. The CDs in solid state displayed a multi-exponential decay (Fig. 5 c) with average lifetime of 1.89 ns and 1.88 ns for excitations at 370 and 500 nm, corresponding to yellow emission (570 nm). There were no significant changes in the lifetimes upon excitation with different wavelengths. Besides, the blank paper too emitted at 430 and 532 nm (λe
x 370 nm) and corresponding lifetime (Fig. 5 b) was recorded. Previous literature has reported higher lifetime values for CDs in their solid state when compared to that in their aqueous solution [73]. However, in the present work, no significant change was observed in the lifetime of blue emitters, whereas the green emitters in the solid state had an extended lifetime of 0.14 ns in the solid state compared to that in solution. The lifetime of the CDs coated on UV-dull paper is independent of the fluorescence lifetime of the blank paper. Table 3 lists the average fluorescence lifetime values of CDs in solution and solid states.

The optimization of structure mimicking the CD analogues in ground and first excited states were performed using B3LYP/6-31G(d,p) theory level. To consider the O:N ratio of ∼10:1 obtained in the experimental studies, polar groups were introduced accordingly to obtain the optimized structures; i) five –COOH and one –NH2 (Fig. 6
aI), ii) four –COOH, two –OH and one –NH2 (Fig. 6 aII), iii) three –COOH, four –OH and one –NH2 (Fig. 6 aIII) and (iv) two –COOH, six –OH, and one –NH2 groups (Fig. 6 aIV) for the calculation. In the structures, the –COOH group is always out of plane with large dihedral angle of 54°, whereas the –NH2 is out of plane by ∼21°. The –OH groups close to the –NH2 group are also out of plane by ∼20°, whereas the –OH groups away from –NH2 are co-planar with the rigid planar frame. The optimizations of the structures were also performed in the first excited state. It is observed that upon electronic excitation the structure in Fig. 6 bI with five –COOH and one –NH2 groups adopted nearly planar conformation (dihedral angle: 7°) and a small change observed in –COOH group. With increase in the number of –OH groups, the –COOH groups displayed a tendency towards planarity with the carbon ring frame. The excited state structures of above-mentioned CDs are shown in Fig. 6 b.

The theoretical studies provide a means to compare the results with experimental spectra wherein slight deviations can arise due to many factors. The IR spectra of molecules were obtained from the harmonic frequency calculations for the correct identification of the molecular structure. The calculated spectra were compared with the experimental FTIR spectra of the fourth fraction considering solvent (methanol) interactions. The experimentally obtained characteristic peak at 1043, 1112 and 1645 cm−1 of the CDs also appeared in all the four DFT calculated structures with varying intensities as evident from the theoretically calculated spectra (Fig. 6 c). In the first structure (Fig. 6 cI), though the peaks positioned at 1034 cm−1, 1113 cm−1 and 1667 cm−1 appears shifted, they fall within the range of the specific functional groups. Similar observations can be drawn from the second structure (Fig. 6 cII) stating the presence of the speculated groups. Additional peaks are pronounced in the third and fourth structures (Fig. 6 cIII & cIV) due to contributions from numerous hydroxyl groups. Further, experimentally obtained UV spectra were compared with the calculated absorption spectra as shown in Fig. 6 d. DFT calculations were performed for one hundred excited states using vertical excitation. The position of these excited states is shown as vertical black line in the spectra along with their convolution for comparison with the experimental UV–Vis spectra. The absorption spectra collected through experimental method show intense peak at 268 and 315 nm representing the major transitions at higher energy levels. Two transitions were observed in all four structures around 267 and 302 nm for theoretical deduced plots corresponding to (π → π*) and lower (n → π*). The lower energy absorptions are extended in lesser intensity from 400 to 600 nm in the experimental and theoretical spectra, which can also be correlated with the theoretical photoluminescence spectra, which were collected from 500 to 800 nm. Therefore, emission spectra of the CDs were also recorded experimentally for less intense absorptions at 580 and 600 nm to observe corresponding emissions at 600 and 630 nm (Fig. 6 e). The structure I (Fig. 6 eI) and II (Fig. 6 eII) has estimated emission at 700 and 706 nm while structure III (Fig. 6 eIII) and IV (Fig. 6 eIV) show deviations from the recorded emission.

From the above theoretical studies, it can be deduced that FTIR and UV spectra for all four possible structures display similarities, while the first and second structure (Fig. 6 aI and II) can be the closest matches to that of the GABA-derived CDs obtained from the fourth fraction of ML-7. There is probability of existence of all four structures in varying amounts. The optical band gap of 2.6 eV obtained experimentally using Tauc plot as shown in SF9 for the CDs dispersed in methanol is almost in agreement to 2.3 eV obtained using theoretical studies in methanol as solvent. The HOMO-LUMO and the energy gap values of the CDs computed using DFT calculations are listed in ST2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Metal ions induced fluorometric responses of CDs
ce:section.ce:para:
There are several reports on the impact of metal ions on the emission of CDs, either enhancement or quenching of fluorescence [74,75]. Therefore, the CDs solution was exposed to different metal ions (100 μL, 0.1 M) and the respective fluorescence spectra were collected as presented in Fig. 7
a. All the samples showed decrease in fluorescence intensity compared to control (CDs alone), except the CD solutions treated with Na+, K+ and Mg2+, and this quenching effect was remarkably significant in the case of Cu2+ ions as observed in the bar diagram presented in Fig. 7 b. Further, 100 μL of varying concentrations of Cu2+ ions (0.00001, 0.0001, 0.001, 0.01, 0.1 and 1 M) were added to the CDs solution and the respective fluorescence spectra as presented in Fig. 7 c were recorded. The bar diagram as shown in Fig. 7 d indicates that the fluorescence quenching due to Cu2+ ions is prominent from decimolar concentration. Further, the precipitate formed upon adding the cupric ions into the CDs solution was collected, washed with water, and dried. The EDS spectrum (Fig. 7 e) of the product showed the presence of C, N, O, and Cu atoms suggesting a plausible coordination-induced fluorescence quenching mechanism upon addition of Cu2+ ions to CDs. The possible mechanism of Cu2+ induced quenching of fluorescence is presented in Fig. 7 f.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Flexo-ink formulation using CDs as pigment
ce:section.ce:para: Solvent based inks are extensively used in flexography printing but are associated with volatile organic compounds that can be released at any stage including ink manufacture, blending and printing processes. Due to environment concerns, there is a need to replace the solvents in the ink system with either water or vegetable oil. Water based formulations that have better wettability seems fit for paper/board substrates with appreciable absorption capacity [76]. The flexo-ink formulated as mentioned in the experimental section appeared dark yellow in daylight and displayed green fluorescence under UV illumination (SF10). The pigment concentrate with CDs in methanol had an initial pH of 8, while pH reduced to 7.3 upon mixing with other components to form the final ink. The 3D photoluminescence spectrum of the ink was compared with that of the control (ink formulation without GABA-derived CDs). The 3D spectra of control (SF11a) formulation displayed emission centred at 390 nm (in UV region labelled as A) and 411 nm (labelled as B) at 330 nm excitation. As observed from SF11b, the ink formulation emitted at 470 and 538 nm (labelled as A) for excitation at 400 and additional emission at 490 and 538 nm (labelled as B) for excitation corresponding to 460 nm. Thus, the presence of CDs creates profound changes in the emission of the formulation, and the ink was further used for laboratory printing through flexography technique.

The optical properties of the ink film were initially analysed by coating it on a UV dull paper via draw down technique. The coated sample appeared as a pale-yellow patch in daylight with yellow fluorescence under a UV light source. Further, solid patches of the ink were printed on UV dull paper using RK Flexiproofer. The ink layers dried quickly under ambient conditions displaying yellow fluorescence under UV illumination. The excitation and emission spectra of the blank, coated, and printed paper samples were recorded. The spectral region is confined between 300 and 530 nm (marked as A in SF12, including excitation and emission) in the blank paper, hence the emissions beyond 530 nm is attributed to CDs. Upon excitation at 370 nm, coated sample exhibits emission at 440 and 532 nm which arises due to paper itself. However, a small hump is observed (Fig. 8
a inset) at 570 nm, and the emission becomes prominent upon receiving excitation at 520 nm. Similarly, a prominent peak (Fig. 8 b inset) was observed at 570 nm for excitation at 370 nm for the printed sample. Thus, a characteristic yellow fluorescence from the CDs is observed for both coated and printed samples. Moreover, the fluorescence emission intensities of coated and printed samples stored under ambient conditions remained the same (SF13a & b) even after 5 months, suggesting the excellent stability of the ink film.

The International Commission on Illumination recommends the use of 3D CIELAB colour spaces, which closely mimics the response to human eye to monitor variation of colour of printed sample. The L*, a* and b* are used to describe a colour. The L* value that describes the image luminescence and lightness to human eye ranges from 0 to100 [40]. The values of other two chromatic components range between −120 to 120; negative and positive values correspond to green and red for a*, whereas they represent blue and yellow for b*. In addition, the ΔE value ranging between 0 and 2 indicates that the print is invisible, those lying between 2 and 3 can be deciphered by a colour specialist and if greater than 3 can be recognized by everyone. As per ISO 12647–6 standards, the acceptable colorimetric parameters of L* ≥90, a* ≤3, and b* ≤5 are specified for a paper substrate in flexographic printing.

The UV dull paper used in the present study was 0.121 mm thick with density, L*, a* and b* values as 0.13, 91.79, −0.64 and 4.85, respectively (ST3). The density, L*, a* and b* values were measured as 0.24, 84.81, 2.78 and 8.61 for the printed samples. The shift of L* to a lower value indicated slight yellowing, while the positive a* and b* values suggested reddening and yellowing of the printed paper sample. Further, the photostability was checked by passing a printed sample of 1.0 cm × 1.0 cm dimension through UV curing unit at 300 W/30 m/min for 0, 10, 20, and 30 passes. The emission spectra in SF14a depicts that the peak monitored at 570 nm due to CDs did not induce any drastic decline in the fluorescence of the printed sample upon different cycles of UV exposure. Similarly, the fluorescence spectra recorded for the same sample after 5 months (SF14b) also displayed minimal changes in the emission region centred at 570 nm. The ST3 lists the densities, L*, a*, b* and ΔE values of UV exposed prints. The L* values of the exposed samples are closer to the printed sample, which indicated the photostability of the print towards UV irradiation. Moreover, a* and b* values remain positive inferring red and yellow colours, respectively. The calculated ΔE values point towards the visibility of the printed ink as the value is greater than 3.

Furthermore, to test the adherence of ink film onto the UV dull paper and its resistance to scuffing, abrasion test that mimics the kind of damage that could result from brushing paper against a print was performed. The experiment was conducted by attaching 8 cm × 5 cm print to the rubber pad mounted on the basal plate. The rub resistance was measured by placing a weight of 2 lbs test block rubber pad over the printed paper. The tester was adjusted for 50 and 100 rubs, and the density and colour measurements recorded after each set of strokes are listed in ST3. The ink film displayed moderate rub resistance as there was no appreciable difference in the colorimetric and density values compared to the unrubbed print.

Security inks enable documents/products authentication and fraud detection. The GABA-CDs ink displayed UV light induced yellow fluorescence, which can be easily replicated by a forger using other pigments/dyes. Incorporation of unique authentication features that are known only to the user and masked from the forger is of high demand to protect the integrity of genuine documents/goods. In this context, stimuli-responsive CDs that display changes in fluorescence when exposed to solvents, chemicals, pH, mechanical force/pressure, or temperature can be useful as smart functional nanomaterials for anticounterfeiting applications, which can accomplish improved security features. As the Cu2+ ions could significantly quench the fluorescence of GABA-derived CDs, which are adorned with CO, COOH, and OH surface groups, the ink film could serve as an excellent authentication platform to bind with cupric ions influencing the emission characteristics. Therefore, the prints were exposed to 2 drops of varying concentrations of Cu2+ ions and allowed to dry. The emission spectra (λex = 370 nm) were recorded for the respective paper samples as portrayed in the inset of Fig. 8 c. Interestingly, the yellow emission from the coats due to CDs at 570 nm (λex = 520 nm) showed a significant concentration dependent quenching pattern (Fig. 8 d) with minimum detection limit in the decimolar range. The negatively charged surface functional groups of CDs can effectively coordinate with Cu+2 to demonstrate a non-radiative recombination resulting in fluorescence quenching [77].

Based on the above cupric ion induced turn-off fluorescence response of CDs, ionochromic papers were designed for encrypting information/stamping using Cu2+ aqueous solution as the ink. The paper was coated with GABA-derived CDs, which appeared colourless in daylight and exhibited yellow fluorescence under 365 nm UV light. Further letter ‘V’ was handwritten on the paper using 0.1 M aqueous cupric ion solution. The letter was not visible in daylight but could be read as a non-fluorescent marking on the yellow-fluorescent background under UV light. This anticounterfeiting technology can be used for data storage, encryption and decryption purposes as shown in SF15.

Intelligent flexible authentication labels for tracing and authentication of pharmaceuticals, premium beverages, consumer goods and products can enable protection. Flexible printed electronics on paper substrates for electronic-anticounterfeiting application has opened new and unique avenues to safeguard genuine items. Uniform print with smooth surface topologies and suitable electronic features is desired for security inks used in these applications. Therefore, the surface morphology and the electrical properties of the printed paper sample are analysed. Using topological scans produced by the AFM method, the roughness of the printed surfaces was evaluated. The several surface grooves observed on the blank paper (SF16a) were covered by ink film in the case of printed sample as depicted in the SF16b. Additionally, the root mean square roughness (Rq) and average roughness (Ra) of the surface profile parameters offer a quantitative way to evaluate the quality and characteristics of the surface. The Ra of unprinted and printed samples demonstrated an average roughness of 0.834 mm and 0.632 mm, respectively. The more sensitive Rq values obtained for the unprinted and printed samples were 227.0 nm and 187.8 nm, respectively, which provide evidence for a smoother printed surface. Further, the printed sample was subjected to Hall Effect measurements to examine its electrical properties. The Hall coefficient value was recorded as −1284.29 cm3/C. In addition, other parameters including mobility of 16.9519 cm2/Vs, carrier density of 4.86 × 1015 cm−3, electrical conductivity of 13.199 × 10−5 Ω−1m−1, resistance of 75.7607 × 10−2 Ωm and bulk conductivity of 52.796 × 10−5 Ω−1m−1 were also measured. The smoother and homogenous surface topographies and the electronic properties of the water-based GABA-derived CD inks could be further explored for electronic anticounterfeiting applications.

This study reports the successful synthesis of CDs via hydrothermal method using amino and carboxyl groups comprising GABA as the precursor material. Column chromatography was adopted to separate the CDs fraction with desired fluorescence emission. The time resolved spectra depicted biexponential decay indicating the presence of two fluorophores: blue and green emitters. The negatively charged carbonaceous nanoparticles decorated with several surface functional groups were characterised using various spectroscopic and microscopic techniques. The DFT studies were performed to generate the different plausible structures as well as the electronic and vibrational spectra of the GABA-CDs. The band gap computed from the theoretical HOMO-LUMO levels was in agreement with the optical band gap realized experimentally. The CDs appeared fluorescent green in its methanolic solution, whereas a yellow emission was perceived from the solid form when coated on paper substrate. This property was well-harnessed to formulate a water-based UV-fluorescent ink and further a homogenous ink film was obtained on paper substrate using flexography. The ink film displayed good light fastness, stability, and abrasion resistance. Moreover, the printed paper samples displayed promising application in document/product authentication. The quenching of yellow fluorescence of the print when in contact with cupric ions can be a unique feature for identifying the genuine product, whereas this feature will be masked to the forger. In addition, Cu2+ ion induced fluorescence quenching can be utilized for potential data storage applications in high security ionochromic papers. Moreover, the surface morphological and electrical parameters studied could be further explored for electronic anticounterfeit applications.
